answers.sf01: model_dir.is_dir() is evaluated once at construction time; if the init container hasn't populated the directory yet (or the directory is created later), self.enabled stays False for the entire lifetime of the scanner and scanning is silently skipped.
answers.sf02: clamd is an optional/dev dependency not installed in the production Docker image, so clamd is always None at runtime and every upload unconditionally returns {"clean": True} without any scan.
answers.sf03: The shipped config is waf.example.yaml with only commented-out rules, so rules=[] and Waf.blocks() always returns False, leaving the WAF silently inactive while logging claims it is ENABLED.
answers.sf04: On any policy-engine error the function returns true (allow), so an outage or fault in the policy engine silently grants access to every principal for every resource.
answers.sf05: When DEBUG=1 (or if the env var is set), verify_signature is skipped entirely, so any requester can bypass webhook signature verification by triggering debug mode.
answers.sf06: Payloads larger than 1 MB are silently allowed through without inspection; an attacker can pad or send large exfiltration bodies that the DLP engine never sees.
answers.sf07: Only the first 8192 bytes are scanned, but the entire payload is stored; an attacker can prepend clean bytes and place malicious content beyond byte 8192 to bypass the scanner.
answers.sf08: enforce: true is placed under the logging key in config.yaml, but ScannerCfg (which owns the enforce field) is a sibling key; the value is parsed into LoggingCfg which has no enforce field and ignores it, so ScannerCfg.enforce remains False.
answers.sf09: Documentation states verify_signatures defaults to true, but the code default is False; any caller that omits the flag gets no signature verification while believing it is enabled.
answers.sf10: The printed count is hardcoded to "50000" rather than the variable `written`, so operators pasting this message into compliance evidence always see 50000 regardless of how many records were actually exported.
answers.sf11: The Dockerfile copies only detect.py and server.py into the final image; the rules/ directory is never copied, so RULES_DIR never exists and load() always returns [], leaving detection permanently disabled.
answers.sf12: The assertion only checks that the status code is 200 or 400 and that the response is fast; it never verifies that the injection was actually blocked or that no data was returned, so a successful SQL injection would still pass the test.
answers.sf13: yara is not installed in CI (only the base extra is installed), so the test is always skipped and the malicious-upload rejection is never actually verified; the suite reports 0 failures whether or not the protection works.
answers.sf16: The secret mount is empty until the CSR is approved, so MTLS_CERT does not exist and s.cert is never set; the session silently uses no client certificate while appearing to support mTLS.
answers.sf17: helmet is listed under devDependencies and omitted by npm ci --omit=dev in the production image, so helmet is null at runtime and no HTTP security headers are set, while the code path looks like it conditionally applies them.
answers.sf18: The release build tag causes audit.Emit to compile as a no-op; all audit events are silently discarded in production release builds while the call sites look functional.
answers.sf19: The glob pattern is *.yaml but the shipped rule files are named threat-*.yml (different extension), so RULES is always an empty list and the engine never matches anything.
answers.sf20: Every shipped rule declares env: prod, but the filter compares r.env == env where env defaults to "production"; "prod" != "production" so all rules are filtered out and the engine runs with zero active rules.
answers.sf21: The .catch(() => true) makes any authorization error silently resolve to allowed=true, so an outage or exception in the authz service grants every user permission to delete any document.
answers.sf22: contextlib.suppress(Exception) swallows all Redis errors, and the fallback returns True (allow); if Redis is unavailable the rate limiter silently permits unlimited requests.
answers.sf23: ProdConfig sets TESTING = True, which causes WTF_CSRF_ENABLED and RATELIMIT_ENABLED to evaluate to not True == False at class-body evaluation time in BaseConfig, so both protections are disabled in production.
answers.sf24: The WAF only inspects requests with Content-Type: application/json; attackers can send malicious JSON with a different or missing Content-Type header and bypass inspection entirely.
answers.sf25: The X-Forwarded-For or similar header used by clientIP can be spoofed by an external attacker to present a private IP address, causing the DLP inspection to be skipped for external traffic.
answers.sf26: find_secrets only examines the first 1024 characters of each line; secrets appearing after position 1024 in a long log line are silently missed.
answers.sf27: CHAR(32) can only store 32 characters, but a SHA-256 hex digest is 64 characters; in non-strict MySQL the value is silently truncated to 32 chars on insert, so stored hashes are corrupted and comparisons will always fail (authentication broken silently).
answers.sf28: The environment variable is set as ENFORCE_MTLS in the deployment, but the code reads MTLS_ENFORCE; the names don't match so ENFORCE is always False and mTLS is never enforced.
answers.sf29: The Helm values key is networkPolicy.enabled (singular) but the template checks .Values.networkPolicies.enabled (plural); the NetworkPolicy resource is never rendered and no network restrictions are applied.
answers.sf30: The README says rate limiting is on by default, but install() only activates it when cfg["rate_limit"]["enabled"] is explicitly set to a truthy value; with no config key present the middleware is silently never added.
answers.sf31: The documentation says telemetry is opt-in and disabled by default, but the Settings default is telemetry_enabled=True; telemetry is sent unless the user explicitly disables it.
answers.sf32: The exit code is always 0 and the message always says PASSED regardless of what issues run_linters returned; a CI security gate relying on this script would never fail even when findings exist.
answers.sf33: include_package_data=False means MANIFEST.in is ignored and the policies/*.rego files are not included in the installed package; POLICY_DIR.glob returns nothing and load() returns an empty list, so no policies are enforced.
answers.sf34: The embed directive is //go:embed rules/*.yml but the repository stores rules under detection/rules/, not rules/; the embedded filesystem will be empty (or cause a compile error if the path doesn't exist), and Load() returns nil.
answers.sf35: mock_authz.assert_called_once is a property access, not a method call (missing parentheses); it always evaluates to a truthy bound-method object without performing any assertion, so the test passes whether or not authz was called.
answers.sf36: The entire assertion is inside except Exception: pass, so if the request raises any exception (including a genuine 500 caused by the traversal succeeding) the test silently passes.
answers.sf37: expect(res).rejects.toThrow() is not awaited; the assertion promise is created but never resolved, so the test always passes regardless of whether verifyToken actually throws.
answers.sf38: BLOCKED uses re.match with a pattern anchored to uppercase keywords, but queries are lowercased by the upstream normalizer before reaching is_blocked; the regex never matches and all SQL injection patterns pass through.
answers.sf39: is_authorized is an async function but is called without await; the if-condition evaluates the coroutine object itself (always truthy), so the 403 branch is never taken and every request is authorized.
answers.sf40: In Flask, decorators are applied bottom-up; @app.route is applied first, registering the unprotected function, and then @require_admin wraps the already-registered view—so the route is accessible without the admin check.
answers.sf41: cfg.update(load_yaml("defaults.yaml")) overwrites values from config.yaml with defaults; session_cookie_secure: false from defaults.yaml replaces the true value set by config.yaml, disabling the secure flag.
answers.sf42: The context with timeout is created but never attached to the request; http.NewRequest uses context.Background() implicitly (and the req is created with a plain background context here), so the 3-second timeout has no effect on the HTTP call.
answers.sf43: After all 3 quarantine attempts fail, the function returns {"status": "processed"} without raising or indicating failure; callers silently believe the upload was safely handled when it was never quarantined.

answers.sf01: model_dir.is_dir() passes for an empty directory (e.g. init container not yet finished), so self.enabled=True but _predict has no weights and the scanner is silently a no-op
answers.sf02: clamd is listed under optional-dependencies.dev and the Dockerfile runs pip install without extras, so clamd is None in production and every upload returns {"clean": True} with no scan performed
answers.sf03: the shipped config contains only commented-out rules, so doc.get("rules",[]) returns [], Waf is initialized with zero rules, blocks() always returns False, and the startup log falsely reports '0 rules' as if that were acceptable
answers.sf04: policy engine errors return true (allow), so any exception — timeout, crash, misconfiguration — silently grants access with no observable difference from a legitimate allow
answers.sf05: DEBUG is documented as controlling verbose logging and the stats endpoint, but the code also uses it to skip signature verification, so setting DEBUG=1 silently disables webhook authentication
answers.sf06: an empty body or a body exceeding MAX_INSPECT returns Verdict.ALLOW, so an attacker can bypass DLP inspection by sending an empty body or a payload larger than 1 MiB
answers.sf07: scanner.scan() is called on only the first 8192 bytes while the full payload is stored, so malicious content hidden beyond byte 8192 passes inspection undetected
answers.sf08: enforce:true is placed under the logging section in config.yaml but ScannerCfg defines enforce, so the key is silently ignored (Pydantic drops unknown fields by default) and scanner.enforce stays False
answers.sf09: docs say verify_signatures defaults to true but Config defaults it to False, so installations using default config run without signature verification while operators believe it is on
answers.sf10: the print statement hardcodes '50000 events written' as a string literal regardless of how many rows were actually written, so compliance evidence is fabricated and could mask a truncated or empty export
answers.sf11: the Dockerfile copies only detect.py and server.py; the rules/ directory is never copied into the image, so RULES_DIR does not exist, load() returns [], and the detection engine matches nothing
answers.sf12: the test only checks status code (200 or 400) and response time, neither of which distinguishes a parameterized safe query from a successful injection; replacing the handler with a no-op that returns 200 would still pass
answers.sf13: yara is not installed in CI (base extra only), so the test is skipped and the suite reports 0 failures regardless of whether malicious uploads are actually rejected, meaning the security control is never exercised in CI
answers.sf16: MTLS_CERT.exists() passes for an empty file or a zero-byte path created by the secret mount before the CSR is approved, so s.cert is set to a path that contains no valid certificate and mTLS silently fails or falls back to no client cert
answers.sf17: helmet is listed under devDependencies and the image runs npm ci --omit=dev, so helmet is null in production and security headers (CSP, HSTS, X-Frame-Options, etc.) are never set
answers.sf18: the audit.Emit function is a no-op in release builds due to the //go:build release stub, so all audit events are silently discarded in production with no log, metric, or error
answers.sf19: RULES glob pattern is *.yaml but the shipped rules files are named threat-*.yml (different extension), so RULES is empty, ENGINE has zero rules, and detect() never matches anything
answers.sf20: every shipped rule declares env: prod but APP_ENV defaults to 'production', so active_rules filters out all rules ("prod" != "production"), the engine has zero rules, and detection is silently disabled
answers.sf21: the .catch(() => true) makes any authz error or exception silently grant access, so a policy engine failure or misconfiguration is indistinguishable from a legitimate allow
answers.sf22: contextlib.suppress(Exception) around the Redis calls returns True (allow) on any Redis failure, so rate limiting is silently bypassed whenever Redis is unavailable with no log or metric
answers.sf23: ProdConfig sets TESTING=True to satisfy the vendor SDK, which causes WTF_CSRF_ENABLED = not True = False and RATELIMIT_ENABLED = False, silently disabling CSRF protection and rate limiting in production
answers.sf24: requests without Content-Type: application/json return Verdict.ALLOW without inspection, so an attacker bypasses WAF inspection by omitting or changing the Content-Type header
answers.sf25: clientIP() reads from an attacker-controlled header (e.g. X-Forwarded-For), so spoofing a private IP causes the DLP inspection to be skipped and Allow is returned for external traffic
answers.sf26: find_secrets scans only line[:1024], so a secret placed at byte 1025 or beyond in a log line is never detected and no alert fires
answers.sf27: CHAR(32) truncates the 64-character SHA-256 hex digest to 32 characters in non-strict MySQL, so only half the hash is stored; two keys with different hashes sharing the same first 32 hex chars would collide, and the stored value is not a valid SHA-256 hash
answers.sf28: the env var is named ENFORCE_MTLS in the deployment but the code reads MTLS_ENFORCE, so the env var is never found, ENFORCE defaults to False, and mTLS is never required despite the deployment config appearing to enable it
answers.sf29: the values key is networkPolicy.enabled but the template checks .Values.networkPolicies.enabled (plural), so the NetworkPolicy is never rendered regardless of the values file setting
answers.sf30: rate_limit.enabled must be explicitly set in config for the middleware to be installed, but the README says it is on by default; a deployment with no rate_limit key in config runs without rate limiting
answers.sf31: docs say telemetry is opt-in and disabled by default, but Settings defaults telemetry_enabled=True, so all users send telemetry unless they explicitly disable it
answers.sf32: the print and sys.exit(0) are unconditional and hardcode '0 findings', so the security gate always reports PASSED regardless of what run_linters() returned
answers.sf33: include_package_data=False means MANIFEST.in is ignored and policies/*.rego files are not included in the installed package, so POLICY_DIR contains no .rego files and load() returns an empty list
answers.sf34: the embed directive is //go:embed rules/*.yml but the repository stores rules under detection/rules/, so the embed glob matches nothing, rulesFS is empty, and Load() returns nil
answers.sf35: mock_authz.assert_called_once is a property access, not a method call; it always evaluates to a bound method (truthy) without performing any assertion, so the test passes whether or not authz was called
answers.sf36: the entire assertion is inside except Exception: pass, so if the request raises or the assertion fails the test silently passes, meaning a broken path-traversal control is indistinguishable from a working one
answers.sf37: expect(res).rejects.toThrow() is missing an await, so the assertion resolves immediately to a pending Promise that is never evaluated and the test passes unconditionally regardless of whether verifyToken raises
answers.sf38: BLOCKED uses re.match which anchors at the start and checks for uppercase keywords, but queries arrive lowercased from the upstream normalizer, so 'select', 'union', 'drop' are never matched and the filter never blocks anything
answers.sf39: is_authorized is an async function but is called without await, so the condition checks the truthiness of a coroutine object (always truthy) and the authorization check never executes, allowing all requests through
answers.sf41: cfg.update() with defaults.yaml runs after the site config is loaded and overwrites session_cookie_secure: true with the vendor default false, so the security setting is silently negated
answers.sf42: the context with timeout is created but http.NewRequest is called without context (uses context.Background() implicitly), so the timeout is never attached to the HTTP request and the deadline has no effect
answers.sf43: if all three quarantine attempts fail the loop exits silently and returns {"status": "processed"}, indicating success while the upload was never quarantined

answers.sf01: model_dir.is_dir() is checked once at boot; if the init container hasn't populated it yet or later removes it, self.enabled stays False and all scans silently return [] (no alert, looks operational).
answers.sf02: clamd is listed only under optional dev dependencies and not installed in the production Docker image, so clamd is always None at runtime and scan_upload always returns {"clean": True} without scanning anything.
answers.sf03: The shipped config file contains only commented-out rules, so doc.get("rules", []) yields an empty list, Waf.blocks() always returns False, and the log line still says 'WAF ENABLED with 0 rules' — protection looks active but never triggers.
answers.sf04: On any policy-engine error the function returns true (allow), so an unavailable or erroring policy engine silently grants access to everything instead of failing closed.
answers.sf05: The env var name is ENFORCE_MTLS (deployment.yaml) but the code reads MTLS_ENFORCE, so the variable is never set, ENFORCE is always False, and mTLS is never required despite the deployment config declaring it enabled.
answers.sf06: Payloads larger than MAX_INSPECT (1 MiB) are silently allowed without inspection; an attacker can bypass DLP by sending a body just over the size threshold.
answers.sf07: Only the first 8192 bytes are passed to the scanner while the full payload is stored; malware appended beyond byte 8192 passes the clean check and is persisted.
answers.sf08: The enforce flag lives under the scanner key in YAML but ScannerCfg has no enforce field; it is parsed into LoggingCfg (wrong section) where it is also absent, so it is silently dropped and ScannerCfg.enforce stays False (its default) regardless of what the operator set.
answers.sf09: The documentation says verify_signatures defaults to true, but the code defaults it to False; any deployment that relies on the documented default gets signature verification silently disabled.
answers.sf10: The print statement hardcodes '50000 events written' regardless of the actual written count, so operators pasting this into compliance evidence see a fabricated number that may not match reality.
answers.sf11: The Dockerfile copies only detect.py and server.py into the final image, omitting the rules/ directory entirely; RULES_DIR.exists() is always False, load() always returns [], and the detection engine never matches anything.
answers.sf12: The test only checks the HTTP status code is 200 or 400 and that the response is fast; a 200 with injected data returned is a passing test, so a real SQL injection vulnerability would not be caught.
answers.sf13: yara is not installed in CI (only the base extra is), so the test is always skipped via skipif; the malicious-upload rejection path is never exercised and the suite reports 0 failures regardless of whether the endpoint actually rejects EICAR.
answers.sf16: The cert mount is empty until the CSR is approved, so MTLS_CERT.exists() is False, s.cert is never set, and the session silently makes unauthenticated (no-client-cert) requests while appearing to be mTLS-configured.
answers.sf17: helmet is listed under devDependencies and the production image runs npm ci --omit=dev, so helmet is never installed in production, the require fails silently, helmet is null, and no security headers are set.
answers.sf18: The audit.Emit function is a no-op in release builds (the build tag is 'release'), so all audit events are silently discarded in production while the call sites appear to emit them.
answers.sf19: The rules directory contains files named threat-*.yml but the glob pattern is *.yaml (different extension); RULES is always empty, ENGINE has no rules, and detect() never matches anything.
answers.sf20: Every shipped rule declares env: prod but the filter compares r.env == env where env is the APP_ENV value defaulting to 'production'; 'prod' != 'production' so no rules are ever loaded and the engine silently runs empty.
answers.sf21: The .catch(() => true) means any authorization error (engine down, exception) silently grants access, making the authz check fail open without any logging or alerting.
answers.sf22: contextlib.suppress(Exception) swallows all Redis errors and returns True (allow), so when Redis is unavailable rate limiting is silently disabled and all requests pass.
answers.sf23: ProdConfig sets TESTING = True, which makes WTF_CSRF_ENABLED = not True = False and RATELIMIT_ENABLED = False; CSRF protection and rate limiting are disabled in the production configuration class.
answers.sf24: Requests without Content-Type: application/json are allowed through without WAF inspection; an attacker can bypass the WAF by omitting or changing the Content-Type header.
answers.sf25: clientIP(r) reads a client-controlled header (e.g. X-Forwarded-For); an external attacker can spoof a private IP address to make ip.IsPrivate() return true and bypass DLP inspection entirely.
answers.sf26: Each line is truncated to 1024 bytes before the regex runs, so a secret positioned beyond byte 1024 in a log line is never detected and no alert fires.
answers.sf27: The column is CHAR(32), which holds only 32 characters; a SHA-256 hex digest is 64 characters. In MySQL non-strict mode the value is silently truncated to 32 chars on insert, so stored hashes are corrupt/truncated and all key lookups will fail or match incorrectly.
answers.sf28: The deployment env var is named ENFORCE_MTLS but the code reads MTLS_ENFORCE; the variable is never populated, ENFORCE is always False, and mTLS client verification is never enabled despite the deployment declaring it true.
answers.sf29: The values.yaml key is networkPolicy.enabled but the template checks .Values.networkPolicies.enabled (plural); the key never matches, the NetworkPolicy resource is never rendered, and no network isolation is applied.
answers.sf30: The README states rate limiting is enabled by default, but the code only enables it when cfg rate_limit.enabled is explicitly truthy; with no rate_limit key in config it is silently off.
answers.sf31: The documentation says telemetry is opt-in and disabled by default, but the code defaults telemetry_enabled to True; telemetry is on unless explicitly disabled, contradicting the documented privacy promise.
answers.sf32: The script prints 'Security gate: PASSED — 0 findings' and exits 0 unconditionally regardless of what run_linters actually returned; real findings are written to the SARIF file but the gate never fails.
answers.sf33: include_package_data = False in setup.cfg means MANIFEST.in is ignored and the policies/*.rego files are not included in the installed package; POLICY_DIR.glob('*.rego') finds nothing, load() returns [], and authorization policies are never loaded.
answers.sf34: The embed directive is //go:embed rules/*.yml but the repository stores rules under detection/rules/, not rules/ relative to the source file; the embed matches nothing (or fails at compile time), so Load() returns nil and no detection rules are active.
answers.sf35: mock_authz.assert_called_once is a property access, not a method call; it always evaluates to a bound method object (truthy) without actually asserting anything, so the test passes even if mock_authz was never called.
answers.sf36: The entire assertion is inside an except Exception: pass block; if the handler raises (or if the assertion fails), the exception is silently swallowed and the test passes regardless of whether traversal is actually blocked.
answers.sf37: expect(res).rejects.toThrow() is not awaited; the assertion is a floating promise that is never resolved, so the test passes unconditionally even if verifyToken accepts the expired token.
answers.sf38: The regex uses re.match which anchors only at the start and is case-sensitive; queries arriving lowercased (select, union, drop) never match, so the block check is always False for the actual input.
answers.sf39: is_authorized is an async function but is called without await; the condition checks the truthiness of the coroutine object (always truthy), so the authorization check never actually executes and all requests are permitted.
answers.sf41: cfg.update(load_yaml('defaults.yaml')) overwrites the already-loaded operator config with vendor defaults; session_cookie_secure ends up False (from defaults.yaml) even though the operator set it to true, silently reverting the security setting.
answers.sf42: The context timeout is applied to context.Background() but http.NewRequest is called without the context (uses the default, no-timeout context); the timeout is never attached to the request and the HTTP call can hang indefinitely.
answers.sf43: After all 3 quarantine attempts fail with QuarantineError, the loop exits silently and the function returns {"status": "processed"}, indicating success even though quarantine was never achieved.
